title: App Registrations & Service Principals (identities for software)
summary: Humans aren't the only things that call Azure — apps, scripts, and CI pipelines do too, and they need identities just as much. App registrations and service principals are how non-human software proves who it is.
analogy: An app registration is the blueprint and corporate charter filed at headquarters (the global definition of the app). A service principal is the actual employee badge that charter produces inside one tenant — the thing that swipes through doors and gets permissions.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  AppReg[App registration\nglobal blueprint] -->|instantiated in a tenant| SP[Service principal\nlocal identity]
  SP --> Creds{How it proves itself}
  Creds --> Secret[Client secret\n(a password)]
  Creds --> Cert[Certificate]
  Creds --> Fed[Federated credential\n(keyless / OIDC)]
  SP --> RBAC[Gets RBAC roles\nat a scope]
  RBAC --> Res[Calls Azure resources]
body:
## First principles

Zero Trust says *every* request must come from a verified identity. But most requests in a real system aren't made by humans clicking buttons — they're made by software: a web app reading a database, a nightly script rotating files, a CI pipeline deploying infrastructure. If only humans can have identities, then all that software has to either *borrow a human's* credentials (terrifying) or run with no identity at all (impossible under Zero Trust). So software needs **first-class identities** of its own.

## Why it exists

Before non-human identities, integrations were a horror show of shared passwords. A pipeline deployed using "the deploy account" — a human-style login whose password sat in a config file, was known to six people, never rotated, and had far more access than the job needed. When someone left, nobody dared change it. When it leaked, everything was exposed.

**Service principals** fix this by giving each app or workload its own identity in the directory — one you can scope tightly, grant least-privilege roles, audit independently, and disable without breaking a human's login.

## How it works

Two objects, often confused, work together:

- **App registration (Application object)** — the **global definition** of an application: its name, its sign-in configuration, what permissions it requests, and its credentials. It lives in the tenant where the app was registered and is the *blueprint*. There is exactly one per app.
- **Service principal** — the **local instance** of that app *inside a specific tenant*. It's the concrete identity that actually gets **RBAC role assignments** and is evaluated at sign-in. When you register an app, a service principal is created in your home tenant; if a multi-tenant app is used by another org, a service principal for it is created in *their* tenant too.

So: one app registration (blueprint) → one service principal per tenant that uses it (badge). RBAC roles attach to the **service principal**, not the registration.

How does a service principal *prove* it's the app? Three options, worst to best:

1. **Client secret** — a generated password string. Simple, but it's a secret you must store, protect, and rotate before it expires. If it leaks, you're compromised.
2. **Certificate** — stronger than a secret, but you still manage and rotate it.
3. **Federated credential (workload identity federation)** — *no stored secret at all.* The app presents a token from a trusted issuer (GitHub Actions, AKS, another cloud) and Entra exchanges it for an access token. This is the modern, keyless ideal.

And for software running *on Azure itself*, there's an even better answer — **managed identities** — a special, fully-managed kind of service principal where Azure handles everything. That's the next lesson.

## In practice

```bash
# Create an app registration + its service principal in one step,
# and assign a least-privilege role at a tight scope.
az ad sp create-for-rbac \
  --name "<deploy-pipeline>" \
  --role "Contributor" \
  --scopes "/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/<rg-name>"

# Output includes appId (client ID), password (client secret), and tenant.
# Prefer a federated credential over the secret for CI/CD:
az ad app federated-credential create \
  --id <app-id> \
  --parameters '{"name":"gh-deploy","issuer":"https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com","subject":"repo:<org>/<repo>:ref:refs/heads/main","audiences":["api://AzureADTokenExchange"]}'
```

Scope it to the resource group, not the subscription. Give it Contributor only if it truly needs to create resources; Reader if it only inspects. The whole point is least privilege.

## Gotchas

- **App registration vs service principal is the #1 confusion.** Registration = global blueprint (one). Service principal = per-tenant identity that holds permissions (one per tenant). You assign RBAC to the *service principal*.
- **Client secrets expire — and break things at 2 a.m.** A huge share of "it suddenly stopped working" incidents are expired SP secrets. Prefer certificates or, better, federated credentials so there's nothing to expire.
- **Over-scoping is rampant.** It's tempting to give the pipeline Owner on the whole subscription "so it just works." That's a giant blast radius. Scope to the smallest resource group and the weakest role that does the job.
- **A leaked secret is a real breach.** Never commit SP secrets to source control or bake them into images. If software runs *on* Azure, skip secrets entirely and use a managed identity.
teachItBack: Apps, scripts, and pipelines call Azure too, so they need their own identities instead of borrowing a human's password. An app registration is the global blueprint of an application; a service principal is the concrete per-tenant identity that actually receives RBAC roles. A service principal proves itself with a client secret, a certificate, or — best — a federated credential with no stored secret at all. Always scope it to least privilege, and if the software runs on Azure, use a managed identity so there's no secret to leak or rotate.

glossary.definition: The global definition (blueprint) of an application in Entra ID: its config, requested permissions, and credentials.
The concrete instance of an app's identity within a specific tenant; the object that receives RBAC role assignments.
A generated password a service principal can use to authenticate; must be stored, protected, and rotated.
A keyless way for a workload to authenticate by exchanging a trusted external token for an Entra token — no stored secret.
An identity belonging to software (app, script, workload) rather than a person.
